options: Dedupe on userId so each user's repeated clicks collapse into one
Mint a unique impression ID when the ad is served and dedupe clicks on it
Keep only the first click per user per one-minute tumbling window
Dedupe on the ad creative ID so the same ad counts only once
why: Mint a unique impression ID when the ad is served and carry it on the click event, so retried or duplicate clicks collapse to one. userId or creative ID is too coarse and drops legitimate distinct clicks.

options: Kafka holding the raw HTML, so the parser reads pages directly off the log
A Redis list holding the URL, with workers popping entries via BRPOP
SQS holding just the URL, with visibility timeout giving easy backoff plus a DLQ
A SQL table of pending URLs that workers poll and mark as claimed
why: SQS is picked because its visibility timeout makes retries, exponential backoff, and dead-letter queues easy. Store a small URL pointer, not raw HTML, and keep the message in-flight until the HTML is confirmed stored in S3.

options: CPU spent parsing and tokenizing each downloaded HTML page
DNS resolution, cited at up to ~70% of per-thread time, fixed with DNS caching and multiple providers
Disk I/O writing and reading the frontier queue under high fan-out
Network bandwidth saturating the link to the blob store on writes
why: Per the Mercator paper, DNS can dominate thread time. Cache DNS results and round-robin multiple DNS providers. Crawling is I/O-bound, so a handful of network-optimized machines suffices for the 5-day target.

options: Parse and respect robots.txt, honoring both Disallow rules and Crawl-delay
Rate-limit to roughly 1 request/sec per domain via a per-domain Redis SET NX lock
Add jitter to request timing to avoid synchronized retry storms against a host
Crawl each domain from many IPs at once to maximize throughput per host
why: Politeness means respecting robots.txt including Crawl-delay, throttling per domain to ~1 rps via a per-domain lock, and adding jitter. Hammering one host from many IPs is the opposite of polite.

options: Maintain a global visited-URL set and skip any URL already fetched once
Dedupe by content hash, not just URL, and cap crawl depth at ~15-20 hops from seed
Cap the crawl at a fixed total page count to bound the work per run
Detect traps by limiting the number of path segments in any single URL
why: URL-level dedup misses mirror and duplicate content, so also hash page content. Cap depth as hops from the seed (not path segments) to escape infinite trap pages.

options: A B-tree keyed by item, giving sorted exact counts with logarithmic updates
A Bloom filter per item to test membership before incrementing its counter
A Count-Min Sketch for approximate per-item counts, plus a heap for the top-K
A sorted set per item in Redis, ranked by its running exact count
why: A Count-Min Sketch estimates per-item counts in megabytes instead of a hundreds-of-GB exact hash table; feed the estimates into a heap to keep the top-K. Only valid when precise counts can be relaxed.

options: Sample 1% of views and multiply the resulting counts back up by 100
Ingest via Kafka, shard counts across many DBs, take each shard's top-K, and merge
Round each counter to the nearest thousand to shrink the per-item write load
Count only logged-in users so the per-key write rate fits one database
why: Buffer with Kafka and shard counters; each shard's local top-K, when merged, is mathematically guaranteed to contain the global top-K, so it stays exact without a single hot DB.

options: Sliding windows require continuously re-aggregating overlapping ranges; tumbling stays tractable
Tumbling windows are inherently less accurate, and that lower accuracy is acceptable here
Tumbling windows need no stored state because each window is recomputed on read
Real users only ever request non-overlapping windows in practice anyway
why: Sliding windows force continuous re-aggregation of overlapping ranges; tumbling windows let you keep one simple per-window aggregate (precomputed and cached per minute).

options: In-memory increments are not atomic, so concurrent requests corrupt the counter
The load balancer spreads a client across gateways, so each sees only part of the count; centralize in Redis
Holding counters in RAM at this scale is too expensive versus a disk-backed store
An in-memory counter can only key on IP, which breaks user-based and API-key limits
why: With traffic spread across gateway instances, no single instance has the true count. Centralize the counter in Redis (sharded by client key) so the limit is enforced globally.

options: Run the check, refill, and decrement as one atomic unit via Lua or MULTI/EXEC, plus EXPIRE
Read the token count first, then issue a separate write to decrement it
Take a global lock on the whole Redis instance around each bucket update
Use WATCH on the key and simply retry the whole operation whenever it conflicts
why: Bundle the read of tokens, the refill, and the decrement into one atomic transaction or Lua script so concurrent requests cannot both spend the last token; set EXPIRE so idle buckets do not leak memory.
accept: WATCH/retry (optimistic) is a real Redis pattern, but at high contention it spins and retries; a single atomic Lua script is the cleaner, contention-free choice HI uses.

options: Fail open and allow all requests, since blocking legitimate users is the worse outcome
Fail closed and reject requests, since outages often coincide with spikes and failing open can collapse the backend
Fall back to a per-instance in-memory limiter until Redis recovers
Serve every request from cache and skip the limit check entirely during the outage
why: HI calls both fail-open and fail-closed defensible but picks fail-closed for social media: limiter failures tend to coincide with traffic surges, so failing open could let a flood cascade into a backend collapse.
accept: Fail open is genuinely defensible (it prioritizes availability), but for a social API a coincident spike makes fail-closed the safer default.

options: A per-paragraph lock so only one editor mutates a region; the alternative is last-write-wins
Operational Transformation, as Google uses; CRDTs are the alternative for P2P or huge collaborator counts
Two-phase commit across editor sessions; the alternative is a saga of compensating edits
Last-write-wins on the whole document; the alternative is first-write-wins on conflict
why: OT transforms concurrent INSERT/DELETE ops against each other on a central server so all clients converge, with low memory and great fit for text. CRDTs suit peer-to-peer or very large collaboration but cost more memory.

options: The full document content on every keystroke, letting the server diff it server-side
Edits as operations, such as INSERT or DELETE at a given character position
A periodic snapshot of the visible page so the server can reconstruct changes
Only the final document state when the user explicitly saves the file
why: Transmit compact operations (insert/delete at offsets), not the whole doc. That is what OT transforms and what Cassandra stores append-only, partitioned by documentId.

options: Pin each user to a server by sticky session on their source IP address
Consistent hashing by documentId with a ZooKeeper-maintained ring; client upgrades to WS on the right server
Route every editor of every document to one global stateful server
Random load balancing, then have servers gossip edits to each other
why: Route by documentId via consistent hashing so collaborators converge on one stateful server; ZooKeeper keeps the ring consistent as servers join and leave. Snapshot and compact operations to bound storage and transfer.

options: Treat the timeout as a failure and reissue the charge until one succeeds
Use idempotency keys plus reconciliation against the payment network, never assuming a timeout equals failure
Charge again immediately and issue a refund later if it turns out to be a duplicate
Block all automatic retries and require the user to resubmit the payment manually
why: A timeout is ambiguous because the charge may have succeeded. An idempotency key makes the retry a no-op if it already went through, and reconciliation against the network catches any mismatch.

options: Store only the current balance and status in a single mutable table
Event sourcing: an append-only immutable event log, captured via CDC, not just current state
Write current state to the DB and export daily CSV snapshots for the audit trail
Keep authoritative state in Redis and periodically flush it to durable storage
why: An append-only event log is the foundation for audits, chargebacks, reconciliation, and fraud detection; storing only current state loses the history that regulators and disputes require.

options: Encrypt the card number at rest in your primary database with a managed key
Tokenization: the card is exchanged for a token so your backend never stores or handles the raw number
Hash the card number with SHA-256 before persisting it alongside the order
Keep the raw card number only in append-only logs, never in the live database
why: Tokenize card data (e.g. via the provider's client-side SDK) so raw card numbers never reach your servers, keeping you out of PCI scope. Pair it with API keys and request signing for security.

options: An API gateway or load balancer as the request entry point
A primary database holding the core entities
A cache such as Redis in front of hot reads
A CDN for static assets like images, JS, and video
A graph database as the primary store for every entity
why: The standard product-design skeleton is clients to an API gateway/LB to stateless services to a primary DB, with a cache for hot reads and a CDN for static media. A graph DB is situational, not baseline.

options: Start from the data model, then derive APIs and services from the schema
Requirements (functional and non-functional), then core entities, API, high-level design, then deep dives
Sketch the full architecture upfront, then back-fill the justification for each piece
Do capacity estimation first, then let the numbers dictate every component choice
why: HI's framework: nail requirements (functional plus non-functional), define core entities, sketch the API, build the high-level design, then go deep on the hard parts and trade-offs.

options: The availability-versus-consistency priority for the system
Latency targets for the key user operations
Expected scale and throughput for both reads and writes
Durability needs for the data the system holds
The programming language and framework the team will build in
why: Non-functional requirements (the -ilities) shape every later decision: availability-versus-consistency, latency, throughput/scale, and durability. Language choice rarely changes the architecture.

options: Observability through metrics, logs, and traces
Fault tolerance and elimination of single points of failure
Security and authentication across the system
Horizontal scalability of each tier under load
The team's choice of editor and local tooling
why: Beyond the happy path, name observability, fault tolerance and SPOF removal, security, and how each tier scales horizontally. These are the operational signals interviewers look for at senior+.

options: Add read replicas to the primary database to spread out read load
A CDN caching static assets at edge locations close to users
A message queue to decouple the asset-serving path from the origin
Geo-sharding the primary database by the user's home region
why: A CDN caches static content at edge locations near users, cutting latency and offloading the origin. It is the standard answer for fast global static delivery.

options: Process the encoding synchronously inside the upload request and return when done
Ack the upload immediately, enqueue a job, and have a worker pool process it in the background
Stream the raw upload to a read replica and encode it there off the write path
Hold the connection open and send progress chunks until encoding finishes
why: This is the managing-long-running-tasks pattern: validate, enqueue, and return a job ID in milliseconds while separate workers do the heavy processing and update status asynchronously.

options: Run SQL LIKE with a leading wildcard against the posts table in Postgres
Add Elasticsearch, whose inverted index makes full-text search fast; a leading-wildcard LIKE forces a full scan
Add a Postgres GIN trigram index and serve all search from read replicas
Cache each query result in Redis so repeated searches avoid the database
why: Full-text search needs an inverted index from term to documents. Elasticsearch is the go-to; a leading-wildcard LIKE cannot use a B-tree index and scans the whole table.
accept: Postgres GIN trigram indexes do enable real text search and can suffice at smaller scale, but a dedicated search cluster is the standard answer for millions of posts with ranking and relevance.

options: A write/create service that generates the unique short code
A read/redirect service that resolves a code to its long URL
A key-value store mapping short code to long URL
A cache, since reads dominate writes roughly 1000 to 1
A video transcoding pipeline for uploaded media
why: Bitly decomposes into a write path (generate a unique code, store the mapping), a read/redirect path, a KV store for the mapping, and a cache because reads vastly outnumber writes.

options: Blob storage such as S3 for the file contents
A metadata database for files, versions, and ownership
A presigned-URL service for direct client upload and download
A sync and notification service to propagate changes to devices
A full-text search cluster indexing the contents of every file
why: Core pieces: blob storage for bytes, a metadata DB for file/version/share info, presigned URLs so clients transfer directly to S3, and a sync/notification path to keep devices consistent.

options: An event and search service backed by Elasticsearch for browsing events
A booking service on an ACID database to prevent double-booking
A seat-reservation mechanism using a distributed lock with a TTL
A payment integration to charge for confirmed bookings
A real-time ML pipeline that retrains seat-recommendation models continuously
why: Ticketmaster needs search (ES), a transactional booking service (ACID prevents double-booking), a reservation hold (Redis lock with TTL), and a payment integration. Continuous model retraining is not core.

options: A post service plus a durable post store
A follow/social-graph store, with a reverse index for follower lookups
A feed-generation path using fan-out workers or precomputed feeds
A feed cache to serve hot reads quickly
A distributed lock service to serialize feed writes
why: Feed systems need posts, the follow graph (with a reverse index), feed generation (fan-out on write or read or a hybrid), and a feed cache. A distributed lock is not part of the core feed path.

options: Stateful WebSocket chat servers holding persistent client connections
A pub/sub routing layer to reach the right server, e.g. Redis Pub/Sub per user
An inbox or message store for delivering to offline recipients
A presence and last-seen mechanism
An OLAP data warehouse sitting on the core message-delivery path
why: Chat needs persistent WebSocket servers, a routing layer so a message reaches whichever server holds the recipient's connection, durable inboxes for offline users, and presence. OLAP is analytics, not the live path.

options: A location service with a geospatial index, e.g. Redis geohashing
A ride-matching service with a lock to avoid double-dispatch
A maps, ETA, and fare integration
A push-notification service via APNs and FCM
A full-text search cluster over driver and rider profiles
why: Uber needs high-frequency location ingestion plus proximity search (geospatial index), matching (with a lock so one driver is not double-assigned), maps/fare, and notifications. Full-text search is not core.

options: A streaming ingestion layer such as Kafka or Kinesis
A stream processor for windowed aggregation such as Flink
An OLAP store for advertiser analytics queries
A data lake on S3 plus batch Spark jobs for reconciliation
A transactional RDBMS as the primary aggregation store
why: The pipeline is stream ingestion to Flink aggregation to OLAP for queries, plus a batch layer (S3 + Spark) to reconcile and serve as source of truth. A row-store RDBMS cannot absorb the aggregation write rate.

options: A frontier queue of URLs to fetch, backed by SQS
Fetcher workers plus a content-extraction stage
Blob storage on S3 for raw HTML and extracted text
A metadata DB for dedup and crawl state
A DNS resolver with caching
A WebSocket server pushing crawl results to connected clients
why: A crawler needs a frontier queue, fetch and extract workers, blob storage for content, a metadata DB (URL/content dedup plus state), and DNS resolution. WebSockets serve live clients, not batch crawling.

options: Stateful WebSocket document servers holding editor connections
A concurrency-control engine using Operational Transformation
An append-only operations store such as Cassandra
Coordination to route editors of one doc to one server, e.g. a ZooKeeper hash ring
A CDN that serves the live, continuously-edited document to editors
why: Real-time editing needs WebSocket servers, an OT engine to converge concurrent edits, an append-only ops store, and coordination (consistent hashing by docId via ZooKeeper). A CDN cannot serve constantly-mutating live state.

options: An API gateway handling auth and request signing
A payment/intent service plus its database
A transaction service that talks to external payment networks
An append-only event log (event sourcing) for audit and reconciliation
A full-text search cluster over transaction descriptions
why: Payments need a secure gateway, a payment-intent service plus DB, a transaction service to external networks, and an immutable event log enabling audit, reconciliation, and chargebacks. Search is not core.

options: A cache in front of the database to absorb abusive read traffic
A rate limiter at the edge (API gateway), keyed per user, IP, or API key
A read replica to spread the load that abusive clients generate
A larger load balancer with more capacity to absorb the flood
why: Put a rate limiter at the edge (API gateway/LB) so abusive traffic is rejected before it loads downstream services; key it per user, IP, or API key for fairness.

options: Open a long-running database transaction that holds the row until checkout completes
A distributed lock with a TTL, e.g. Redis, that auto-releases if the user abandons
Optimistic concurrency: let anyone proceed and reject the loser at final commit
A status column flipped to reserved, swept back by a periodic cleanup job
why: A distributed lock with a TTL holds the resource for a bounded time and auto-releases if the user abandons. It is the standard reservation pattern (Ticketmaster, Uber dispatch).
accept: A status column with a sweeper can work, but it relies on a separate cleanup job to reclaim abandoned holds; a TTL lock self-expires, which is simpler and more robust.

options: Short polling, where the client re-requests on a fixed interval
Long polling, where the server holds each request open until data arrives
Server-Sent Events, a one-way server-to-client push stream
WebSocket, a single persistent full-duplex connection
why: WebSocket gives full-duplex, low-latency bidirectional messaging over one persistent connection, which is right for chat and collaboration. SSE is one-way; polling adds latency and overhead.

options: Store the video bytes as BLOBs in the primary database alongside metadata
Store videos in blob storage (S3) fronted by a CDN, keeping only the URL in the DB
Chunk videos across multiple database rows and reassemble them on read
Hold videos in Redis as binary values and persist with append-only snapshots
why: Large binaries go in blob storage (S3) fronted by a CDN; the DB stores just the URL/reference. Binaries in the DB wreck index and cache efficiency and cost far more, and Redis is volatile memory.

options: The transactional row store that already ingests the raw events
An OLAP columnar store such as ClickHouse, Druid, or BigQuery
A key-value cache holding precomputed totals for each advertiser
Blob storage queried directly with on-demand scan jobs per request
why: Analytical aggregation over huge event volumes belongs in an OLAP/columnar store optimized for scans and group-bys, separate from the transactional ingest path. A row store cannot answer these in sub-second time.

options: SQS, where each message is deleted after a single consumer acks it
Kafka with a separate consumer group per team, each tracking its own offset
A shared database table the teams poll and delete rows from after processing
RabbitMQ with one queue and competing consumers across the three teams
why: Kafka retains the log and lets multiple consumer groups read it independently, each with its own offset. Queue models where a message is consumed once cannot fan the same stream out to three teams.

options: A composite B-tree index on the latitude and longitude columns
A geospatial index such as Redis geo, PostGIS, or Elasticsearch geo-queries
A full-text search index over location names and addresses
A two-step filter: bound by a lat range, then by a long range, then intersect
why: Proximity search needs a geospatial index (geohash or quadtree based). A B-tree (even bounding by each axis separately) cannot efficiently answer 2D radius queries.

options: A relational database with a leader-election table updated under a transaction
A coordination service like ZooKeeper for leader election, config, and failure detection
A message queue where the first consumer to claim a message becomes leader
A shared Redis key with SET NX as the leader lock for the whole cluster
why: Distributed coordination, including leader election, consistent configuration, and failure detection, is ZooKeeper's job, using a hierarchical znode namespace plus watches with strong consistency (ZAB).
accept: A Redis SET NX lock can elect a leader in simple cases, but it lacks ZooKeeper's failure detection, watches, and strongly-consistent config storage that production coordination needs.
